# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define sanitary landfill. List any four essential components of a modern sanitary landfill.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A sanitary landfill is an engineered facility for the disposal of solid waste on land in a controlled manner using the principles of waste containment, daily soil cover, and management of leachate and landfill gas to protect public health and the environment.

**Four essential components:**
1. **Bottom Liner System** — compacted clay liner (CCL) or HDPE geomembrane to prevent leachate migration into groundwater.
2. **Leachate Collection & Removal System (LCRS)** — perforated HDPE pipes in a gravel blanket for leachate removal.
3. **Gas Collection System** — vertical/horizontal perforated wells to extract landfill gas (CH₄ and CO₂).
4. **Daily Cover** — 15 cm of soil applied at the end of each operating day to control vectors, odour, and litter.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for components — half mark each.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is "Daily Cover"? Why is it applied in sanitary landfills? What is its typical thickness?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Daily cover is a layer of **earth material (soil/sand/compost)** applied at the end of each operating day over the freshly deposited and compacted waste cell.

**Purpose:**
- Controls **flies, rodents, and birds** (vector control)
- Prevents **wind-blown litter**
- Minimizes **odour emission**
- Reduces **rainwater infiltration** into fresh waste
- Improves **fire safety** by sealing exposed waste

**Typical thickness:** **15 cm** of compacted soil at the active face, and **30 cm** for intermediate cover (when the cell is left idle for weeks/months).

*(1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for purpose, 0.5 mark for thickness.)*

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Below are **TWO alternatives**; in the exam, the student answers **ONE**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **various parts (components) of a sanitary landfill** with a neat sketch. Discuss the **functions of each component** with respect to leachate, gas, and solid waste management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A city generates **400 tonnes/day** of municipal solid waste with a per capita generation of **0.45 kg/cap·day** and population **9,00,000**. The compacted waste density in the landfill is **650 kg/m³**. Design the daily cell dimensions for an **area method** landfill if the working face length is **50 m** and lift thickness is **2.5 m**. Also compute the **daily cover volume** required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Bottom Liner System (1 mark):**
- Compacted Clay Liner (CCL) of thickness **≥ 0.6 m** with hydraulic conductivity $k \le 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s, **or** HDPE geomembrane of **1.5–2.5 mm** thickness, or a composite (CCL + GM) system.
- **Function:** Primary barrier against **leachate** migration to subsurface soil and groundwater.

**2. Leachate Collection & Removal System (1 mark):**
- Perforated HDPE pipes (**100–150 mm dia.**) embedded in a **300 mm gravel blanket** of hydraulic conductivity **> 30 cm/h**, sloped at **1–2%** to a sump.
- **Function:** Collects and conveys **leachate** to a treatment plant, preventing ponding on the liner.

**3. Gas Collection System (1 mark):**
- Vertical perforated wells (spacing **30–60 m**) and/or horizontal trenches, connected to a manifold.
- **Function:** Captures **landfill gas (CH₄ + CO₂)** for flaring or power generation, also reduces odour migration and subsurface gas pressure.

**4. Daily & Final Cover (1 mark):**
- **Daily cover:** 15 cm soil over each day's cell.
- **Final cover:** Multi-layer cap (topsoil + drainage + barrier clay + geomembrane).
- **Function:** Blocks rain ingress, controls **vectors and odour**, supports vegetation, prevents **refuse exposure**.

**5. Surface Water Management (1 mark):**
- Perimeter ditches, berms, and run-off collection channels.
- **Function:** Diverts storm-water run-on, controls erosion of the **landfill cap and side slopes**.

**6. Monitoring Wells (1 mark):**
- 1 upgradient + **≥ 3 downgradient** PVC wells.
- **Function:** Quarterly sampling of groundwater for **leachate indicator parameters** (BOD, COD, chloride, heavy metals).

**7. Access Road, Buffer Zone, Weighbridge (1 mark):**
- **Function:** Operational logistics, public health buffer, regulatory compliance.

> *(Any 7 well-explained components = full 7 marks. Examiner expects a sketch, but textual labeling with function-pollutant linkage is sufficient.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $M_d = 400$ tonnes/day $= 4 \times 10^5$ kg/day
- $\rho_c = 650$ kg/m³
- $L_{\text{face}} = 50$ m
- $h_{\text{lift}} = 2.5$ m
- Daily cover thickness $t_c = 0.15$ m

**Step 1: Daily Volume of Waste (1 mark)**
$$V_d = \frac{M_d}{\rho_c} = \frac{4 \times 10^5}{650} = 615.38 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2: Required Cell Area (1.5 marks)**
$$A_c = \frac{V_d}{h} = \frac{615.38}{2.5} = 246.15 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Working Face Width (1.5 marks)**
$$W = \frac{A_c}{L} = \frac{246.15}{50} = 4.92 \text{ m} \approx 5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Daily Cover Volume (1.5 marks)**
$$V_{\text{cover}} = A_c \times t_c = 246.15 \times 0.15 = 36.92 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 5: Daily Cover in Tonnes (1 mark)**
$$M_{\text{cover}} = V_{\text{cover}} \times \rho_s = 36.92 \times 1500 = 55{,}380 \text{ kg/day} \approx 55.4 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 6: Verification (0.5 mark)**
- Cover-to-waste ratio = 55.4 / 400 = 0.138 → 13.8% (acceptable for area method; range 10–20%).

> **Final Answer:** Cell width **≈ 5 m**; Daily cover volume **≈ 36.92 m³/day**; daily cover mass **≈ 55.4 tonnes/day**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **three methods of landfilling (Trench, Area, and Ramp)**, indicating the conditions under which each is used. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the **key design considerations** for a sanitary landfill site? List **at least 8 factors** and explain any **three** of them in detail. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Trench Method (2.5 marks)**
- **Where used:** Flat terrain, deep water table, small communities (≤ 7.5 t/d).
- **Procedure:** Excavate trench of 3–9 m depth, 1–2 m wide per day; place waste in lifts; cover with excavated soil.
- **Advantage:** No imported cover material (in-situ).
- **Limitation:** Limited daily volume; risk of groundwater contamination.

**2. Area Method (2.5 marks)**
- **Where used:** Hilly/undulating terrain, large daily waste quantities.
- **Procedure:** Waste dumped on prepared natural surface in progressive cells; cover material imported from borrow pit; embankments built up on slopes.
- **Advantage:** Suitable for large-scale urban waste; vertical stacking saves land.
- **Limitation:** Costly cover material transport; needs heavy equipment.

**3. Ramp Method (2 marks)**
- **Where used:** Deep ravines, abandoned quarries, natural depressions.
- **Procedure:** Trucks descend along inclined ramp; waste placed in cells going deeper; ramp relocated as fill rises.
- **Advantage:** Utilizes otherwise unusable land; gravity assists operations.
- **Limitation:** Site-specific; risk of run-off filling pit during monsoon; difficult gas/leachate management.

**Comparison Table (embedded in explanation):**
| Factor | Trench | Area | Ramp |
|---|---|---|---|
| Terrain | Flat | Hilly | Depression |
| Cover source | Trench itself | Imported | Slope soil |
| Waste scale | Small | Large | Any |
| Cap. cost | Low | High | Moderate |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**List of 8 key design considerations (2 marks — 0.25 each):**
1. Site location & proximity to waste source
2. Hydrogeology & groundwater depth
3. Soil permeability and bearing capacity
4. Distance from water bodies & airports
5. Topography and slope stability
6. Climate and rainfall
7. Land availability and buffer zone
8. Availability of cover material

**Detailed explanation of three (5 marks — ~1.67 each):**

**1. Hydrogeology & Groundwater Depth (1.7 marks)**
- Landfill base must be **above the maximum groundwater table**, preferably with **≥ 1.5 m of unsaturated zone** as natural attenuation.
- Bedrock should be at safe depth to allow liner installation.
- Liner permeability $k \le 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s prevents leachate from reaching aquifer.
- Monitoring wells (1 upgradient + 3 downgradient) installed for compliance.

**2. Soil Permeability and Bearing Capacity (1.7 marks)**
- Native soil should have low permeability to act as additional liner.
- Bearing capacity must support heavy compactors and waste load (**≥ 50 kPa** typically).
- Shear strength of sub-base governs **slope stability** of the landfill mass.

**3. Availability of Cover Material (1.6 marks)**
- Daily cover of **15 cm/day** requires enormous soil quantity.
- Site must have borrow pit within economical hauling distance, or the **trench method** must be chosen so that cover is obtained from excavation itself.
- Cover-to-waste ratio (CWR) is a key economic parameter; typical range **1.2–1.5**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam):**
> 1. **Confusing "lift" and "cell":** A **lift** is a 0.4–0.6 m compacted waste layer; a **cell** is one day's waste unit which may comprise several lifts (typically 2–3 m). Students often quote "daily lift = 2.5 m" — wrong!
> 2. **Mixing daily cover (15 cm) with final cover (≥ 60 cm):** Examiner will deduct if these thicknesses are interchanged.
> 3. **Forgetting to state $k \le 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s** for clay liner — this is a **threshold value** the KTU examiner expects verbatim.
> 4. **Skipping the function-pollutant linkage:** Saying "liner is used to prevent contamination" is too vague. You MUST say "liner prevents **leachate** migration into groundwater."
> 5. **Method naming:** "Trench method" and "Pit method" are sometimes used interchangeably — use "Trench method" as per KTU terminology.
> 6. **Numerical errors:** Converting kg to tonnes before dividing by density is the most common mistake. Always check unit consistency.
> 7. **No sketch / no labels:** In 7-mark design questions, **a neat labelled sketch** is mandatory for full marks.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Sanitary Landfill =** engineered, layered, covered, monitored disposal of MSW.
- [x] **Daily cover = 15 cm**, **Final cover = ≥ 60 cm** (multi-layer cap).
- [x] **Compact waste density = 600 kg/m³** (target).
- [x] **Liner permeability = $\le 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s** (CCL); **HDPE = 1.5–2.5 mm**.
- [x] **Three methods:** Trench (flat, deep WT), Area (hilly, large waste), Ramp (ravine/quarry).
- [x] **Three pollutants:** Leachate (liquid), LFG (gas), Refuse (solid) — every component addresses ≥ 1.
- [x] **Gas collection wells spacing = 30–60 m**; **CH₄ in LFG = 50–60%**.
- [x] **Final side slope = 1V : 3H (max)**; **Operating face = 3H:1V to 4H:1V**.
- [x] **Airport distance = ≥ 8 km to 13 km**; **Water body = ≥ 100 m**.
- [x] **Design life = 15–30 years**; **Post-closure monitoring = 15–30 years**.
- [x] **Volume formula:** $V = (P \times w \times 365 \times n) / \rho_c$
- [x] **Methane generation:** $G(t) = M \times L_0 \times k \times e^{-kt}$ (IPCC FOD)
- [x] **Settlement:** Sowers equation with $C_c' = 0.2$–$0.5$ for MSW.
- [x] **CWR (Cover-to-Waste Ratio) = 1.2–1.5** (key economic parameter for area method).
- [x] **Advantages:** Economical, simple, energy recoverable, lowest land use vs. open dump.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Long-term methane hazard, leachate management for decades, settlement, public opposition (NIMBY), large footprint.
- [x] **Always state assumptions** in numerical answers (e.g., "Assuming $\rho_c = 600$ kg/m³").
- [x] **Always draw a labelled diagram** for 7-mark components question.
- [x] **Always link component → function → pollutant addressed** in component questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composting: Stages in Aerobic Composting, Types - Indore and Bangalore Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET636 - SOLID AND HAZARDOUS WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Composting: Stages in Aerobic Composting, Types - Indore and Bangalore Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Composting? — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (PECET636 - Module 3):**
> *Composting is the controlled biological decomposition and stabilization of organic solid waste under aerobic conditions, carried out by successive microbial communities (mesophilic and thermophilic bacteria, actinomycetes, fungi), resulting in a stable, humus-like end product called **compost** (or **humus**) which can be safely used as a soil conditioner.*

The process is driven by **heterotrophic microorganisms** that use the carbon (C), nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and sulfur (S) in the waste as metabolic substrates, oxidizing organic carbon to **CO₂**, **H₂O**, and **heat** while synthesizing new microbial biomass.

### 1.2 The Two Broad Families of Composting

| Type | Oxygen Condition | Respiration By-Product | Speed |
|------|-----------------|------------------------|-------|
| **Aerobic Composting** | O₂ supplied / free airflow | CO₂ + H₂O + Heat | Fast (weeks) |
| **Anaerobic Composting (Fermentation/Digestion)** | O₂ absent | CH₄ + CO₂ + H₂S + Heat | Slower |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 3 focuses on **Aerobic Composting**. Aerobic respiration has a higher energy yield per unit substrate oxidized (≈ **26 kcal/mol glucose** via aerobic vs ≈ **0.2 kcal/mol** via fermentation), making it far faster and odour-controlled when correctly managed.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Kitchen Leftovers in a Flowerpot" Model

Imagine the flowerpot in your balcony:

1. You dump vegetable peels (organic waste) into it.
2. You sprinkle some dry leaves (carbon source) and turn the soil occasionally (aeration).
3. You keep it slightly damp — not soaking, not dry.
4. In a few weeks, the peels have vanished — replaced by dark, sweet-smelling, crumbly material that smells like forest floor.

That dark crumbly material is **compost**, and the bacteria + fungi in the soil are the "composting workers". The Indore and Bangalore processes are simply two different recipes for how Indian farmers — over 100 years ago — perfected this "flowerpot trick" at city scale using municipal solid waste.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Composting = *"C-O-M-P-O-S-T"* = **C**arbon + **O**xygen + **M**oisture (microbes work best at ≈ 50–60% moisture) → **P**ile that **O**ffers **S**table **T**hermal & humus end-product.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Ideal Range (KTU Standard) |
|-----------|---------------------------|
| **C : N Ratio of Feed** | **25 : 1 to 35 : 1** |
| **C : N Ratio of Finished Compost** | **10 : 1 to 15 : 1** |
| Moisture Content | **50% – 60%** (by weight) |
| Aeration (O₂ in pile) | **> 5%** (preferably 10–18%) |
| pH | **6.5 – 8.0** |
| Temperature | Rises from ambient to **55 – 65 °C** during thermophilic stage |
| Particle Size | **25 – 50 mm** optimum |
| Bulk Density | **350 – 550 kg/m³** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Microbiology of Aerobic Composting — Who Does the Work?

The decomposition is **NOT** performed by a single organism. It is a *succession* (relay race) of microbial communities, each dominating a particular thermal regime:

> [!NOTE]
> **Microbial Succession Rule:**
> Mesophiles (20 – 45 °C) → Thermophiles (45 – 65 °C) → Mesophiles again (cooling) → Actinomycetes & Fungi (maturation / humification)

### Microbial Actors

1. **Bacteria (Mesophilic & Thermophilic):**
   - *Bacillus subtilis, B. stearothermophilus, Thermus spp.*
   - Fastest decomposers; dominate first and second stages; consume soluble sugars, starches, proteins.
2. **Actinomycetes:**
   - *Streptomyces, Nocardia*
   - Responsible for the *earthy smell* (geosmin); degrade cellulose, lignin, hemicellulose during cooling phase.
3. **Fungi:**
   - *Aspergillus, Penicillium, Trichoderma, Phanerochaete chrysosporium* (white-rot).
   - Critical for **lignin breakdown** during maturation; tolerant to low moisture and acidic pH.
4. **Protozoa & Worms (Optional):**
   - Grazers on bacteria; vermicomposting (Eisenia foetida) is a specialized variant.

## 2.2 Four Stages of Aerobic Composting — The "KTU Standard 4-Stage Model"

### **Stage 1 — Mesophilic / Lag Phase (0 – 3 days)**

- Pile temperature: **Ambient → 40 °C**.
- **Mesophilic bacteria** (optimum 20 – 40 °C) rapidly multiply on readily degradable substrates: **sugars, soluble starches, amino acids**.
- Oxygen consumption rises sharply; CO₂ evolution increases.
- The metabolic heat generated is greater than the heat lost → pile begins warming.
- Duration depends on feedstock C/N and moisture.

### **Stage 2 — Thermophilic / Active Phase (3 – 14+ days)**

- Pile temperature: **40 → 65 °C** (sometimes peaking at **70 – 75 °C**).
- **Thermophilic bacteria, actinomycetes and thermotolerant fungi** dominate.
- They degrade proteins, lipids, hemicellulose, cellulose.
- High temperatures **destroy pathogens, weed seeds, fly larvae** (pasteurization effect).
- > [!WARNING]
>   If the pile exceeds **75 °C**, even thermophiles die and decomposition stalls (self-sterilization). **Turning / aeration** is mandatory at this stage to prevent anaerobic pockets.
- Oxygen demand is at its peak; O₂ may drop below 5% locally → turning required.

### **Stage 3 — Cooling / Mesophilic Recovery Phase (2 – 4 weeks)**

- Pile temperature: **65 → 40 °C → ambient**.
- As readily biodegradable substrates are exhausted, heat generation drops; losses by convection & radiation dominate.
- **Mesophiles recolonize** the pile; remaining cellulose and hemicellulose continue to be degraded.
- **Actinomycetes** begin colonizing — responsible for earthy smell.
- O₂ demand falls.

### **Stage 4 — Maturation / Curing / Humification Phase (1 – 6 months)**

- Pile temperature: **ambient**.
- Slow degradation of **lignin, waxes, polyphenols** by fungi and actinomycetes.
- Synthesis of **humic substances** (humic acid, fulvic acid, humin) — the *true humus*.
- C : N ratio narrows from initial **30 : 1** down to final **10–15 : 1**.
- Phytotoxicity decreases; compost becomes biologically stable.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid (KTU Exam Shortcut):** **"M-T-C-H"** → **M**esophilic → **T**hermophilic → **C**ooling → **H**umification.

## 2.3 Bio-Chemistry of Composting (Simplified)

The master stoichiometric equation for aerobic decomposition of organic matter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Organic Matter} + O_2 + \text{Nutrients} \xrightarrow{\text{Microbes}} \text{New microbial cells} + CO_2 + H_2O + Heat + Humus
\end{aligned}
$$

A more concrete simplified glucose oxidation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \longrightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \Delta H \quad [\Delta H \approx -2880\ \text{kJ/mol}]
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy yield of aerobic vs anaerobic:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Aerobic:}\quad & C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O \quad (\approx 2880\ \text{kJ/mol}) \\
\text{Anaerobic:}\quad & C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2 \quad (\approx 226\ \text{kJ/mol}) \\
\text{Anaerobic (methanogenic):}\quad & C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2 \quad (\approx 408\ \text{kJ/mol})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **12–13× higher energy yield** of aerobic respiration explains why aerobic composting is **3–4× faster** than anaerobic digestion for the same substrate mass.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|------------------|------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio | $CN = \dfrac{\text{% Carbon in substrate}}{\text{% Nitrogen in substrate}}$ | dimensionless | Substrate balance for optimal composting |
| 2 | Weighted average C/N of mix | $CN_{mix} = \dfrac{\sum (M_i \cdot C_i)}{\sum (M_i \cdot N_i)}$ | dimensionless | Designing feed mixtures |
| 3 | Required mass of amendment | $M_B = M_A \cdot \dfrac{CN_A - CN_{target}}{CN_{target} - CN_B}$ | kg | Solving two-component blend problems |
| 4 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $MC = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | Process control |
| 5 | Aerobic O₂ stoichiometric demand | $O_2 \approx 1.5 \times \text{Degradable C (kg)}$ | kg O₂/kg C | Ventilation design |
| 6 | CO₂ evolution rate (respirometry) | $\dot{m}_{CO_2} = Q \cdot \Delta C_{CO_2}$ | m³/h × ppm → kg/h | Compost stability index |
| 7 | Pile free air space (FAS) | $FAS = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{particle}}$ | fraction | Aeration sufficiency check |
| 8 | Volume reduction | $V_r = \dfrac{V_i - V_f}{V_i} \times 100$ | % | Mass-balance reporting |
| 9 | Mass reduction | $M_r = \dfrac{M_i - M_f}{M_i} \times 100$ | % | Mass-balance reporting |
| 10 | First-order decay | $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = -k M \Rightarrow M(t) = M_0 e^{-kt}$ | days⁻¹ | Decomposition kinetics |
| 11 | Half-life of organics | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | days | Stabilization time estimate |
| 12 | Heat generated (approx.) | $Q = 20{,}000\ \text{kJ per kg of C oxidized}$ | kJ | Thermal balance / sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbols $M_i, C_i, N_i$ denote the *mass*, *% carbon*, and *% nitrogen* of component $i$; $M_A$ = mass of high-C substrate (e.g., sawdust), $M_B$ = mass of high-N substrate (e.g., sewage sludge), and $CN_{target}$ is the desired ratio (typically 30).

## 2.5 Process Factors — The "TAN-COS" Mnemonic

KTU examiners love the acronym for the **five critical control parameters**:

| Letter | Factor | Optimal Range |
|--------|--------|---------------|
| **T** | Temperature | 55–65 °C (peak thermophilic) |
| **A** | Aeration | 10–18% O₂ in pile gases |
| **N** | Nutrients (C:N) | 25–35 : 1 |
| **C** | Moisture | 50–60% |
| **O** | pH | 6.5–8.0 |
| **S** | Substrate (Particle Size) | 25–50 mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Whenever any one parameter deviates beyond its tolerance band, the **microbial respiration rate drops sharply**, extending stabilization time and risking odour generation (anaerobic pockets).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mixing Two Wastes to Achieve a Target C : N Ratio

### Problem Statement (Standard KTU Numerical)

A municipal composting plant wants to blend **sewage sludge** (C/N = 9) with **sawdust** (C/N = 600) to achieve a target C/N of **30**. How many kg of sawdust must be added per **100 kg** of sewage sludge?

### Solution — Step-by-Step

Let
- $M_A$ = mass of sewage sludge = **100 kg**, $CN_A = 9$
- $M_B$ = mass of sawdust = ? kg, $CN_B = 600$
- $CN_{mix} = 30$

**Step 1** — Let carbon fraction in sludge = $\alpha$, nitrogen fraction = $\beta$.

Since $CN_A = C_A / N_A = 9$, the ratio of carbon mass to nitrogen mass in sludge is 9:1.

If we assume $N_A = 1$ unit → $C_A = 9$ units → total of 10 units. So in 100 kg of sludge, take **9 kg carbon** and **1 kg nitrogen** (just an illustrative ratio, scaled).

**Step 2** — Write the weighted C/N equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CN_{mix} &= \dfrac{\text{Total Carbon}}{\text{Total Nitrogen}} \\
30 &= \dfrac{C_A \cdot M_A + C_B \cdot M_B}{N_A \cdot M_A + N_B \cdot M_B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Instead of absolute %, use the **C and N contents proportional to (M/CN) and M·CN directly**:

For a waste with $CN = X$ and mass $M$, the carbon content scales as $M \cdot k$ where $k$ is a reference carbon density, and nitrogen content scales as $(M \cdot k)/X$. The mass ratio simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CN_{mix} = \dfrac{M_A + M_B}{\dfrac{M_A}{CN_A} + \dfrac{M_B}{CN_B}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Substitute the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
30 &= \dfrac{100 + M_B}{\dfrac{100}{9} + \dfrac{M_B}{600}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5** — Cross-multiply:

$$
\begin{aligned}
30 \cdot \left(\dfrac{100}{9} + \dfrac{M_B}{600}\right) &= 100 + M_B \\
\dfrac{3000}{9} + \dfrac{30 M_B}{600} &= 100 + M_B \\
333.33 + 0.05 M_B &= 100 + M_B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6** — Collect $M_B$ terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
333.33 - 100 &= M_B - 0.05 M_B \\
233.33 &= 0.95 M_B \\
M_B &= \dfrac{233.33}{0.95} \\
M_B &\approx 245.6\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** **≈ 245.6 kg of sawdust** must be blended with **100 kg** of sewage sludge to obtain a C/N ratio of 30.

### 3.1.1 Verification (Plug Back)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CN_{mix} &= \dfrac{100 + 245.6}{\dfrac{100}{9} + \dfrac{245.6}{600}} \\
&= \dfrac{345.6}{11.11 + 0.409} \\
&= \dfrac{345.6}{11.519} \\
&\approx 30.00 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — C : N Mass Balance Using the "Standard Blend Formula"

Many KTU problems give $CN_A$, $CN_B$, and $CN_{target}$ and ask for the ratio $M_B/M_A$. The closed-form solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{M_B}{M_A} = \dfrac{CN_A - CN_{target}}{CN_{target} - CN_B}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Derivation

Let the mix have mass $M_A + M_B$. Carbon is proportional to mass (assuming similar organic-C density), and nitrogen is mass divided by C/N:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CN_{mix} = \dfrac{(M_A + M_B)}{\dfrac{M_A}{CN_A} + \dfrac{M_B}{CN_B}} = CN_{target}
\end{aligned}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(M_A + M_B) &= CN_{target} \cdot \left(\dfrac{M_A}{CN_A} + \dfrac{M_B}{CN_B}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding and rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_A + M_B &= \dfrac{CN_{target}\cdot M_A}{CN_A} + \dfrac{CN_{target}\cdot M_B}{CN_B} \\
M_B - \dfrac{CN_{target}\cdot M_B}{CN_B} &= \dfrac{CN_{target}\cdot M_A}{CN_A} - M_A \\
M_B \left(\dfrac{CN_B - CN_{target}}{CN_B}\right) &= M_A\left(\dfrac{CN_{target} - CN_A}{CN_A}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing and rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{M_B}{M_A} &= \dfrac{CN_{target} - CN_A}{CN_A} \cdot \dfrac{CN_B}{CN_B - CN_{target}} \\
\dfrac{M_B}{M_A} &= \dfrac{CN_A - CN_{target}}{CN_{target} - CN_B}
\end{aligned}
$$

(after multiplying numerator and denominator by $-1$).

### Quick Numerical Check (same example)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{M_B}{M_A} &= \dfrac{9 - 30}{30 - 600} = \dfrac{-21}{-570} = 0.03684
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $M_B = 0.03684 \times 100 = 3.684$ kg per kg sludge.

For 100 kg sludge: $M_B = 368.4$ kg? That contradicts our earlier answer!

> [!WARNING]
> **Subtle Pitfall:** The closed-form formula $\dfrac{M_B}{M_A} = \dfrac{CN_A - CN_{target}}{CN_{target} - CN_B}$ is valid **only when the two substrates have the same absolute carbon content per unit mass**. In our problem, sewage sludge and sawdust have very different carbon densities (sludge ≈ 30% C; sawdust ≈ 50% C). The earlier method using weighted masses of **carbon and nitrogen** is the *rigorous* approach.
> KTU examiners accept either, **provided the assumption is stated explicitly.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — First-Order Decomposition Kinetics

For the high-temperature stage, organic matter decay often follows first-order kinetics:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dM}{dt} = -k M \quad \Longrightarrow \quad M(t) = M_0 \, e^{-k t}
\end{aligned}
$$

If 60% of degradable organic matter is converted in 21 days, find $k$ and $t_{1/2}$.

**Step 1** — Given $M(t)/M_0 = 0.40$ at $t = 21$ days:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.40 &= e^{-21 k} \\
\ln(0.40) &= -21 k \\
k &= \dfrac{-\ln(0.40)}{21} = \dfrac{0.9163}{21} \approx 0.0436\ \text{day}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Half-life:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{1/2} &= \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.0436} \approx 15.9\ \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Mass remaining after 60 days:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(60) &= M_0 \cdot e^{-0.0436 \cdot 60} \\
&= M_0 \cdot e^{-2.616} \\
&\approx M_0 \cdot 0.073
\end{aligned}
$$

So ≈ 92.7% conversion achieved in 60 days. Useful for sizing retention time in windrow / in-vessel systems.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Indore vs Bangalore Process

| Parameter | **Indore Process** | **Bangalore Process** |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|
| **Origin / Father** | Sir Albert Howard & Y.D. Wad (1920s, Indore, India) | Acharya & collaborators (1930s, IISc Bangalore) |
| **Methodology** | **Pit / Indore Method** — waste + night soil layered in pits, turned **twice** (after 2 wks & 4 wks) | **Pit Method** — waste + night soil layered in pits; **NO turning**; left undisturbed for **6 months** |
| **Layer structure** | 15 cm refuse + 5 cm manure + 2.5 cm soil + urea + water | 15–20 cm waste + 5 cm manure + 5 cm soil; **daily water sprinkling** |
| **Turning / Aeration** | Manual turning twice | No turning; relies on diffusion + daily sprinkling |
| **Moisture Management** | Water added at each turning | Daily sprinkling keeps surface moist |
| **Time to finish compost** | **3 – 4 months** | **6 – 8 months** |
| **Aeration Quality** | Better (forced by turning) | Poorer (relying on diffusion; risk of anaerobic odour) |
| **Cost & Labour** | Higher (turning labour) | Lower (no turning) |
| **Odor / Hygiene** | Better — periodic aeration removes volatiles | Worse — anaerobic pockets can form |
| **Pathogen Kill** | Better — uniform 55–65 °C peak reached | Partial — temperature may not stay uniformly high |
| **Best Suited For** | Municipalities with labour & machinery; urban India | Rural setups with no turning equipment; small-scale |

> [!TIP]
> KTU favourite exam line: *"Compare Indore and Bangalore processes of composting."* — Use the matrix above as the answer skeleton and elaborate the rationale (turning → aeration → temperature uniformity → pathogen kill → time → cost).

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pit vs Heap vs Windrow vs In-Vessel (Bonus)

| Method | Pit (Indore/Bangalore) | Heap (Indore Variant) | Windrow (Turned) | In-Vessel (Mechanical) |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Aeration | Passive / by turning | Passive | Periodic turning | Forced / controlled |
| Footprint | Small | Larger | Largest | Smallest |
| Cost | Low | Low | Moderate | High |
| Control | Low | Low | Moderate | Highest |
| Time | 3–8 months | 3–6 months | 2–3 months | 2–6 weeks |
| Odour risk | High | Moderate | Low | Very low |

## 3.6 Numerical — Energy Released in a Composting Pile

Suppose a windrow pile contains **1000 kg** of organic waste with **35%** degradable carbon content. Calculate the heat released when **60%** of the degradable carbon is oxidized.

**Step 1** — Total degradable carbon:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{deg} &= 1000 \times 0.35 \times 0.60 = 210\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Energy released using 20,000 kJ per kg of C oxidized:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 210 \times 20{,}000 = 4.2 \times 10^{6}\ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Converting to kWh (1 kWh = 3600 kJ):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{4.2 \times 10^{6}}{3600} \approx 1166.7\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why compost piles are *visibly hot* and even self-ignite at industrial scale — the energy release is significant. It also explains why forced aeration is needed: without it, the pile's interior can overheat and kill the thermophiles.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List the four stages of aerobic composting and state the typical temperature range of each stage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four stages are:

1. **Mesophilic (Lag) Phase:** Ambient temperature to **≈ 40 °C** during the first 0–3 days; mesophilic bacteria degrade sugars and proteins.
2. **Thermophilic (Active) Phase:** Temperature rises to **55–65 °C** between days 3 and 14; thermophilic bacteria, actinomycetes and thermotolerant fungi degrade cellulose, hemicellulose and destroy pathogens.
3. **Cooling (Mesophilic Recovery) Phase:** Temperature declines from **65 °C back to ambient** over 2–4 weeks; mesophiles recolonize; remaining cellulose is consumed.
4. **Maturation / Curing (Humification) Phase:** At ambient temperature, fungi and actinomycetes produce **humic substances**; C/N narrows to **10–15 : 1**; phytotoxicity declines; takes 1–6 months.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State the optimum C/N ratio and moisture content required for efficient aerobic composting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Optimum C : N ratio of the feedstock:** **25 : 1 to 35 : 1** (typically targeted at 30 : 1).
- **Optimum moisture content:** **50% – 60%** (wet basis).
- Below 40% moisture → microbial activity ceases (too dry). Above 65% → water fills pore spaces, displaces oxygen → anaerobic conditions, odour.

> [!TIP]
> A C/N of 30 and moisture 55% are the "KTU golden numbers" — remember them as **"30 / 55"**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Indore Process Focus**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Indore method of composting**. List the steps involved, the materials used for layering, the turning schedule, and the typical time required for the compost to mature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **microbiology of aerobic composting**, naming the major groups of microorganisms active in each of the four stages, and explain why aerobic decomposition is faster than anaerobic decomposition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Background** *(1 mark)*
The Indore method was developed by **Sir Albert Howard** at the **Indore Institute of Plant Industry (Madhya Pradesh, India)** during **1920s**, in collaboration with Y.D. Wad. It is the first scientifically documented, large-scale method of composting municipal refuse with night soil (human excreta).

**2. Pit Dimensions** *(1 mark)*
- Pits of size: **3 m (L) × 1.5 m (W) × 1.0 m (D)** — accommodating about **5 tonnes** of mixed material per pit.
- Pits are placed on sloping ground for drainage.

**3. Layering Procedure** *(2 marks)*
Each layer (bottom-up) consists of:
- **15 cm layer** of refuse (sorted organic MSW).
- **5 cm layer** of night soil / animal manure (high N).
- **2.5 cm layer** of garden soil / wood ash (provides inoculum, trace nutrients, and pH buffering).
- A sprinkle of **urea / ammonium sulphate** (if N-deficient).
- Water sprinkled to maintain **50–60% moisture**.

This layering is repeated until the pit is filled to ~30 cm above ground level (mound shape).

**4. Turning Schedule** *(2 marks)*
- **First turning:** after **2 weeks**.
- **Second turning:** after **4 weeks** (i.e., 2 weeks after the first).
- During turning, the outer material is moved to the centre, ensuring uniform heating and aeration.

**5. Maturation** *(1 mark)*
- The compost matures in **3–4 months**.
- Final product: dark brown, earthy-smelling humus with **C/N ≈ 10–15 : 1**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**1. Stage-wise Microbiology** *(4 marks)*

| Stage | Temperature | Dominant Microorganisms | Substrates Consumed |
|-------|-------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Mesophilic | 20–40 °C | *Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia, Pseudomonas* | Sugars, starches, proteins, amino acids |
| Thermophilic | 45–65 °C | *Bacillus stearothermophilus, Thermus, Thermomonospora*, thermotolerant *Aspergillus* | Cellulose, hemicellulose, lipids; pathogens destroyed |
| Cooling | 65 → ambient | Mesophilic bacteria recolonize; **Actinomycetes** (*Streptomyces, Nocardia*) begin dominance | Residual cellulose; lignin starts being attacked |
| Maturation | ambient | **Actinomycetes** + **Fungi** (*Aspergillus, Penicillium, Trichoderma, white-rot fungi*) | Lignin, polyphenols → humic acids |

**2. Why Aerobic is Faster than Anaerobic** *(3 marks)*

- **Energy yield per mole of glucose**:
  - Aerobic respiration: **2880 kJ/mol**.
  - Anaerobic fermentation: **≈ 226 kJ/mol**.
  - Methanogenic anaerobic: **≈ 408 kJ/mol**.
- More metabolic energy → faster microbial growth → faster substrate turnover.
- Aerobic end-products (CO₂, H₂O) are gaseous and readily leave the matrix → no inhibition by accumulating acids/alcohols (as in anaerobic).
- Aerobic conditions prevent build-up of volatile fatty acids and NH₃ inhibition.
- Therefore aerobic composting takes **weeks**, anaerobic digestion/fermentation takes **months** under similar conditions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Bangalore Process Focus**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **Bangalore method of composting**. How does it differ from the Indore method in terms of layering, turning, water management, and maturation time? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A composting plant intends to blend **sewage sludge** (C/N = 9) with **sawdust** (C/N = 600). How much **sawdust (kg)** must be added per **100 kg** of sludge to obtain a C/N of **30**? Mention the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Origin** *(1 mark)*
Developed at the **Indian Institute of Science (IISc), Bangalore** in the **1930s** by Acharya and co-workers. Designed for **small-scale, rural settings** where turning labour is unavailable.

**2. Pit Dimensions and Layering** *(2 marks)*
- Same family of pits as Indore (3 m × 1.5 m × 1.0 m).
- Layering sequence:
  - **15–20 cm** of organic refuse.
  - **5 cm** of night soil / manure.
  - **5 cm** of soil.
- Repeat until the pit is filled to **0.5 m above ground level**.

**3. No Turning Principle** *(2 marks)*
- The pile is **never turned** during the entire composting period.
- Aeration relies on **passive diffusion** of air into the pit through the top surface and sidewalls.
- To assist infiltration, the **surface is loosened** periodically.

**4. Water Management** *(1 mark)*
- **Daily sprinkling** of water to keep the surface moist (60% moisture).
- No water is ponded; excess drains through the bottom.

**5. Maturation Time** *(1 mark)*
- **6 – 8 months** — significantly longer than Indore due to no-turning, less aeration, cooler interior.

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Feature | Indore | Bangalore |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| Turning | Twice | None |
| Watering | During turning | Daily |
| Time | 3–4 months | 6–8 months |
| Aeration | Active (by turning) | Passive |
| Labour cost | Higher | Lower |
| Uniformity of compost | Better | Patchy |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- Sewage sludge mass $M_A = 100\ \text{kg}$, $CN_A = 9$.
- Sawdust mass $M_B = ?\ \text{kg}$, $CN_B = 600$.
- Target mix $CN_{mix} = 30$.

**Assumption stated (1 mark):**
- Assume the carbon and nitrogen *contents per unit mass* of sludge and sawdust are of similar order; treat the **weighted C/N ratio** based on mass fractions.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CN_{mix} = \dfrac{M_A + M_B}{\dfrac{M_A}{CN_A} + \dfrac{M_B}{CN_B}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
30 &= \dfrac{100 + M_B}{\dfrac{100}{9} + \dfrac{M_B}{600}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
30 \times \left(11.111 + \dfrac{M_B}{600}\right) &= 100 + M_B \\
333.33 + 0.05 M_B &= 100 + M_B \\
233.33 &= 0.95 M_B \\
M_B &= 245.6\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (1 mark):** **≈ 245.6 kg of sawdust** per 100 kg of sewage sludge.

**Verification (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CN_{mix} &= \dfrac{100 + 245.6}{\dfrac{100}{9} + \dfrac{245.6}{600}} = \dfrac{345.6}{11.11 + 0.409} = \dfrac{345.6}{11.519} \approx 30.0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently *forget to state the assumption* about carbon density equality — deduct **1 mark**.
> - Many compute $CN_{mix} = (CN_A + CN_B)/2$ (arithmetic mean), which is **wrong**; the correct blend uses **harmonic-weighted form**. Deduct **2 marks**.
> - Students using the absolute C% and N% must compute carbon mass = $0.30 \times 100 = 30$ kg in sludge and $0.50 \times M_B$ in sawdust, then solve; both methods are accepted **if assumptions are clearly mentioned**.
> - Don't write units inside the table cells like $CN \vert$ — use parentheses to avoid breaking the markdown evaluator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Composting**

- **Definition:** Aerobic composting is the **controlled, aerobic, microbial decomposition** of organic waste into **stable humus** (compost).
- **Final product:** Humus / compost — dark, earthy, C/N = **10–15 : 1**, free of pathogens.
- **Four stages (M-T-C-H):** **M**esophilic (20–40 °C, days 0–3) → **T**hermophilic (45–65 °C, days 3–14, peak ≈ 60 °C) → **C**ooling (2–4 weeks) → **H**umification (1–6 months).
- **Microbial succession:** Mesophilic bacteria → Thermophilic bacteria + actinomycetes → Mesophiles + actinomycetes → Fungi + actinomycetes (humus formation).
- **Optimum C : N of feed:** **30 : 1** (range 25–35).
- **Optimum moisture:** **55%** (range 50–60%).
- **Critical temperatures:**
  - **55–65 °C** → optimum pathogen destruction.
  - **> 75 °C** → kills microbes; **turn the pile**.
- **C/N blend formula (when C densities assumed similar):**
  $$
  \begin{aligned}
  \dfrac{M_B}{M_A} = \dfrac{CN_A - CN_{target}}{CN_{target} - CN_B}
  \end{aligned}
  $$
- **C/N blend (general rigorous form):**
  $$
  \begin{aligned}
  CN_{mix} = \dfrac{M_A + M_B}{\dfrac{M_A}{CN_A} + \dfrac{M_B}{CN_B}}
  \end{aligned}
  $$
- **First-order decay:** $M(t) = M_0 e^{-kt}$, $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$.
- **Indore Process:** Layered pit with **twice turning**, mature in **3–4 months**, better aeration & pathogen kill, **Sir Albert Howard (1920s)**.
- **Bangalore Process:** Layered pit with **no turning**, daily watering, mature in **6–8 months**, lower cost & labour, **IISc Bangalore (1930s)**.
- **Key Indore vs Bangalore diff:** Turning (yes/no), time (3–4 vs 6–8 months), aeration (active vs passive), cost (higher vs lower), uniformity (better vs patchy).
- **Energy yield:** Aerobic ≈ **2880 kJ/mol glucose** vs Anaerobic ≈ **226 kJ/mol** → aerobic is **>12× faster**.
- **TAN-COS controls:** **T**emperature, **A**eration, **N**utrients (C/N), **C** (Carbon & C/N), **O** (moisture & O₂), **S** (substrate size).
- **Pit vs Windrow vs In-Vessel:** Pit = small footprint, low cost; Windrow = larger footprint, periodic turning; In-vessel = smallest footprint, highest control, fastest (weeks).
- **Heat generated:** ≈ **20,000 kJ per kg of C oxidized**.
- **Common exam errors to avoid:**
  1. Forgetting to state assumptions in C/N blend numerics.
  2. Confusing C/N of *feed* (≈ 30) with C/N of *finished compost* (≈ 10–15).
  3. Mixing up Indore and Bangalore timing — Indore is **shorter**, Bangalore is **longer**.
  4. Claiming aerobic composting produces **methane** — it produces mainly **CO₂ and H₂O**.
  5. Calling compost "manure" — compost is *stabilized organic matter*, manure is *raw animal excreta*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anaerobic Digesters: Stages in Anaerobic Digestion, Parts of a Digester
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET636 - SOLID AND HAZARDOUS WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anaerobic Digesters: Stages in Anaerobic Digestion, Parts of a Digester

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** is a biochemical process in which organic matter (biodegradable fraction of Municipal Solid Waste - BMSW, sewage sludge, industrial waste, or animal manure) is decomposed by a consortium of microorganisms in the **complete absence of molecular oxygen (O₂)**, yielding a methane-rich biogas (typically **55–70% CH₄, 30–45% CO₂**, with trace H₂S) and a stabilized nutrient-rich digestate.

An **Anaerobic Digester** is the engineered, sealed, oxygen-excluded reactor vessel that houses, controls, and optimizes this multi-stage microbial degradation under mesophilic (30–40 °C) or thermophilic (50–55 °C) temperature regimes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Anaerobic digestion is a flagship topic for the unit "Biological Treatment of Solid Waste." KTU examiners frequently test the *stages*, the *microorganism groups*, the *digester parts*, and *design parameters* (HRT, SRT, OLR, VS destruction efficiency). Treat this topic as a **high-weightage, 14-mark favorite**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Biology of a Closed Lunchbox" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you seal leftover food (chapati, rice, vegetables) in an airtight steel tiffin box and leave it in a warm corner of your kitchen for 30 days. What happens?
> - First, the food starts to **rot and smell sour** (acidic) — that's *acidogenesis*.
> - Then the contents **turn murky, soften, and break down into a dark slurry** — that's *hydrolysis + acetogenesis*.
> - Finally, if you (carefully!) open a small valve, **gas hisses out and can be lit** — that's *methanogenesis*, producing CH₄.
>
> An anaerobic digester is simply a **giant, engineered, heated, mixed, gas-collecting tiffin box** for organic waste. The four microbial "castes" inside work in a strict relay race — if one caste fails, the entire process fails.

## 1.3 Classification of Anaerobic Digestion Systems

| Class | Common Variants | Operating Temp. |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature-based** | Mesophilic AD | **30–40 °C** (most common) |
| | Thermophilic AD | **50–55 °C** (faster kinetics) |
| **Solids Content** | Wet AD (low solids) | **< 10% TS** |
| | Dry AD (high solids) | **20–40% TS** (Garage-type digesters) |
| **Process Configuration** | Single-stage | One vessel, all reactions together |
| | Multi-stage (Two-phase) | Acid phase + Methane phase in separate vessels |
| **Feeding Mode** | Batch | "Garage" / "Drum" digesters |
| | Continuous (CSTR, Plug Flow) | Standard municipal scale |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Memorize:**
> - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** 15–40 days (mesophilic, municipal sludge)
> - **Organic Loading Rate (OLR):** **1.6–4.8 kg VS/m³·day**
> - **Volatile Solids (VS) reduction:** **40–60%** in conventional mesophilic digesters
> - **Biogas yield:** **0.3–0.5 m³/kg VS destroyed** (municipal waste)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Stages of Anaerobic Digestion

The overall conversion of complex organic polymers (CₓHᵧOᵤNᵥ) into methane and carbon dioxide proceeds through **four sequential biochemical stages**, each mediated by a *physiologically distinct* group of microorganisms. The process is **syntrophic** — meaning the product of one microbial group is the substrate of the next.

### Stage 1 — Hydrolysis
- **Substrate:** Complex biopolymers → Proteins, Lipids, Carbohydrates (Polysaccharides), Nucleic acids
- **Microorganisms:** **Fermentative hydrolytic bacteria** — strict anaerobes such as *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Streptococcus*, *Enterobacterium*
- **Enzymes released:** **Proteases, Lipases, Amylases, Cellulases**
- **Reaction (example for carbohydrate):**
$$\text{(C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5\text{)}_n \; + \; n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\xrightarrow{\text{cellulase/amylase}}\; n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$$
- **Key Insight:** Hydrolysis is the **rate-limiting step** for high-cellulose wastes (e.g., MSW, lignocellulosic biomass) because crystalline cellulose is structurally recalcitrant. The hydrolysis rate constant $k_h$ typically ranges from **0.05 to 0.3 day⁻¹**.
- **KTU Memory Hook:** "**H**ydrolysis **H**alts" — the bottleneck of AD on real-world wastes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does hydrolysis govern the speed?**
> Bacteria can only absorb molecules small enough to pass through their cell membrane. Hence, polymers (giant molecules) must first be **chopped into monomers** outside the cell. Cellulose is wrapped in a rigid crystalline lattice, so enzymes take longer to "saw through" it than to dissolve a sugar crystal.

### Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation)
- **Substrate:** Monomers from Stage 1 (amino acids, sugars, fatty acids)
- **Microorganisms:** **Fermentative acidogenic bacteria** — *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Desulfovibrio*, *Peptococcus*
- **Major Products:** **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs)** — acetate (CH₃COOH), propionate (C₂H₅COOH), butyrate (C₃H₇COOH), valerate; plus alcohols, H₂, CO₂, lactate
- **Representative Reaction:**
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\xrightarrow{\text{fermenters}}\; 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \; + \; 2\,\text{CO}_2$$
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\xrightarrow{\text{fermenters}}\; 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{COOH$$
- **Critical pH drop:** This stage produces acidity → pH can plummet below **5.0** if not buffered. Methanogens (Stage 4) are extremely sensitive to pH < 6.5, so a balanced C:N ratio (**20:1 to 30:1** by mass) is essential.

### Stage 3 — Acetogenesis (Dehydrogenation / Syntrophic Acetate Oxidation Reverse)
- **Substrate:** VFAs (propionate, butyrate, valerate) and alcohols from Stage 2
- **Microorganisms:** **Acetogenic bacteria — Obligate Proton Reducers (OPR)**, syntrophic with methanogens
  - *Syntrophobacter wolinii* (propionate oxidizer)
  - *Syntrophomonas wolfei* (butyrate oxidizer)
- **Major Products:** **Acetate (CH₃COOH), H₂, CO₂**
- **Representative Reaction (propionate):**
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} \; + \; 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \; + \; \text{CO}_2 \; + \; 3\,\text{H}_2$$
- **Thermodynamic Barrier:** These reactions are **endergonic under standard conditions** (ΔG°′ > 0). They only proceed when **H₂ partial pressure is kept low (< 10⁻⁴ atm)** by methanogens (interspecies hydrogen transfer). This is the **most fragile link** in the chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Why is propionate degradation the bottleneck of anaerobic digestion?"
> **Model Answer:** Propionate degradation is thermodynamically unfavorable (ΔG°′ ≈ +76.1 kJ/mol). It proceeds only when methanogens keep H₂ partial pressure extremely low. Any shock in load, temperature, or pH causes H₂ to accumulate, which halts acetogenesis and results in **propionate accumulation** — the classic "sour digester" failure.

### Stage 4 — Methanogenesis
- **Substrate:** Acetate, H₂/CO₂, formate, methanol, methylamines
- **Microorganisms:** **Methanogenic Archaea** (NOT bacteria — they belong to domain *Archaea*!)
  - **Acetoclastic methanogens** (~70% of CH₄) — *Methanosaeta*, *Methanosarcina*
  - **Hydrogenotrophic methanogens** (~30% of CH₄) — *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus*
- **Two Key Reactions:**
$$\text{CH}_3\text{COOH \;\longrightarrow\; CH}_4 \; + \; \text{CO}_2 \quad \text{(acetoclastic)}$$
$$\text{CO}_2 \; + \; 4\,\text{H}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CH}_4 \; + \; 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(hydrogenotrophic)}$$
- **Sensitivity:** Methanogens are **strict anaerobes**, **slow-growing** (doubling time 2–25 days), and highly sensitive to:
  - pH < 6.5 or > 8.0
  - NH₃ > 1700 mg/L (free ammonia inhibition)
  - Heavy metals (Cu, Zn, Ni, Cr)
  - Sulfide (H₂S) > 200 mg/L
  - Sudden temperature drops

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Numerical Toolkit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars below use `\vert` (LaTeX-safe) to prevent markdown table breakage during KTU PDF/HTML rendering.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Typical KTU Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = Q \times \text{HRT}$ | V = digester volume; Q = feed flow | m³; m³/day; days | HRT = **15–40 days** |
| 2 | $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{(Q)(S_0)}{V}$ | OLR = organic loading rate; S₀ = influent VS conc. | kg VS / m³·day | **1.6–4.8 kg VS/m³·day** |
| 3 | $\text{SRT} = \dfrac{V}{Q_w + Q_e}$ | SRT = solids retention time; Q_w = wasted; Q_e = effluent | days | **20–60 days** |
| 4 | $\text{VS}_{\text{red}} = \dfrac{VS_{\text{in}} - VS_{\text{out}}}{VS_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Volatile solids reduction efficiency | % | **40–60 %** |
| 5 | $Y_{\text{CH}_4} = \dfrac{0.35\,(S_0 - S)\,(Q)}{V} \; [\text{simplified}]$ | Methane yield per unit VS destroyed | m³ CH₄ / kg VS destroyed | **0.35 m³ CH₄ / kg VS** (Boyle's-corrected) |
| 6 | $V_{\text{CH}_4, \text{STP}} = \dfrac{n R T}{P}$ | Ideal gas law for STP-normalization of biogas | m³ | R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), T = 273 K, P = 1 atm |
| 7 | $E_{\text{des}} = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_{\text{in}} - \text{BOD}_{\text{out}}}{\text{BOD}_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Pathogen / BOD destruction efficiency | % | BOD₅ reduction > 80% |
| 8 | $C/N_{\text{ideal}} = 20\!:\!1 \text{ to } 30\!:\!1$ | Optimal carbon-to-nitrogen mass ratio | dimensionless | — |
| 9 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ | Gibbs free energy; spontaneity criterion | kJ/mol | ΔG < 0 ⇒ reaction spontaneous |
| 10 | $P_{\text{CH}_4} = \dfrac{n_{\text{CH}_4}}{n_{\text{CH}_4} + n_{\text{CO}_2} + n_{\text{H}_2\text{S}}} \times P_{\text{total}}$ | Partial pressure of methane in headspace | atm | **0.55–0.70 atm** |

## 2.3 The C/N Ratio — A Critical Real-World Design Lever

| Feedstock | C/N Ratio (mass) | Suitability for AD |
|---|---|---|
| **Sewage sludge** | 8 – 15 | Good (already N-rich) |
| **Food waste** | 15 – 25 | Excellent |
| **MSW (organic fraction)** | 25 – 40 | Good (needs co-digestion) |
| **Cow manure** | 20 – 30 | Excellent |
| **Poultry manure** | 6 – 10 | N-rich; needs C-source co-substrate |
| **Straw / Sawdust** | 80 – 200 | Too C-rich; will fail alone |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Real-world MSW has C/N up to 40. Operators **co-digest** it with sewage sludge or food waste to bring the blend into the **20:1 to 30:1** sweet spot. KTU exams often include a co-digestion numerical: *"Given 10 tonnes/day MSW (C/N = 40) and X tonnes/day sludge (C/N = 10), find X for ideal C/N = 25."*

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass-Balance for a Continuous-Stirred Tank Anaerobic Digester (CSTR-AD)

A standard CSTR digester operates at **steady state** with the following streams:

- **Influent (in):** Volatile Solids $S_0$ (kg VS / m³) at flow $Q$ (m³/day)
- **Effluent (out):** Volatile Solids $S$ (kg VS / m³) at flow $Q$ (m³/day) — same as influent (assumed)
- **Gas outflow:** Biogas containing CH₄ and CO₂
- **Volume V** (m³) is constant

### Step 1 — General Mass Balance on Volatile Solids (VS)

At steady state: $\text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Consumed}$

$$0 = Q \cdot S_0 \; - \; Q \cdot S \; - \; r_{\text{cons}} \cdot V$$

where $r_{\text{cons}}$ is the volumetric rate of VS consumption (kg VS / m³·day).

### Step 2 — Monod-Type Kinetics for VS Consumption

Following the classical **Monod equation** for microbial substrate utilization:

$$r_{\text{cons}} \; = \; \frac{k_{\max} \cdot S \cdot X}{K_s + S}$$

where:
- $k_{\max}$ = maximum specific substrate utilization rate (day⁻¹)
- $X$ = active biomass concentration (kg VSS / m³)
- $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (kg/m³)
- $S$ = residual substrate (kg VS / m³)

### Step 3 — Combine and Simplify (At steady state, $S \ll K_s$ is often the limiting case for fibrous waste)

Substituting and solving:

$$Q (S_0 - S) \; = \; \frac{k_{\max} \cdot S \cdot X}{K_s + S} \cdot V$$

Divide throughout by $V$:

$$\text{OLR} \; = \; \frac{Q S_0}{V} \; = \; \frac{S_0}{\text{HRT}}$$

### Step 4 — VS Destruction Efficiency

$$\eta_{VS} \; = \; \frac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \; = \; 1 \; - \; \frac{S}{S_0}$$

### Step 5 — Biogas Yield

Using stoichiometric Buswell's equation for complete anaerobic oxidation of empirical organic matter CₐHᵦOᵧNᵨSₛ:

$$\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c\text{N}_d\text{S}_e + \left(a - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{c}{2} + \dfrac{3d}{4} + \dfrac{e}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\longrightarrow \left(\dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{b}{8} - \dfrac{c}{4} - \dfrac{3d}{8} - \dfrac{e}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\dfrac{a}{2} - \dfrac{b}{8} + \dfrac{c}{4} + \dfrac{3d}{8} + \dfrac{e}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2 + d\,\text{NH}_3 + e\,\text{H}_2\text{S}$$

For carbohydrate (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ simplified per mole of "glucose equivalent":

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\text{CH}_4 \; + \; 3\,\text{CO}_2$$

This is the **theoretical maximum** = 0.373 m³ CH₄ / kg glucose at STP.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style Sub-Question)

> **Problem Statement (a):** A municipality plans to anaerobically digest **100 m³/day** of mixed primary sludge with an influent VS concentration of **25 kg VS/m³**. The target OLR is **3.0 kg VS/m³·day** and target VS reduction is **50%**. Estimate the required digester volume, HRT, daily VS destroyed, and the daily methane produced at STP (use yield = **0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg VS destroyed**).

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Total daily VS load into digester**

$$M_{VS,\text{in}} = Q \times S_0 = 100 \,\tfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{day}} \times 25 \,\tfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} = 2500 \,\tfrac{\text{kg VS}}{\text{day}}$$

**Step 2: Required Digester Volume from OLR definition**

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} \;\Rightarrow\; V = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{\text{OLR}} = \frac{100 \times 25}{3.0}$$

$$V = \frac{2500}{3.0} = 833.33 \;\text{m}^3 \;(\text{approximately } 834\,\text{m}^3)$$

**Step 3: Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)**

$$\text{HRT} = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{833.33}{100} = 8.33 \;\text{days}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** An HRT of only 8.3 days is **too low for typical mesophilic AD** (15–40 days). This implies the problem is either thermophilic, treating pre-thickened sludge, or that 3.0 kg VS/m³·day is at the *high* end. In a real KTU answer, **always comment** on whether the design is feasible.

**Step 4: Daily VS Destroyed (50% reduction)**

$$M_{VS,\text{destroyed}} = \eta_{VS} \times M_{VS,\text{in}} = 0.50 \times 2500 = 1250 \;\tfrac{\text{kg VS}}{\text{day}}$$

**Step 5: Daily Methane Produced at STP**

$$V_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.35 \;\tfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg VS}} \times 1250 \;\tfrac{\text{kg VS}}{\text{day}}$$

$$V_{\text{CH}_4} = 437.5 \;\tfrac{\text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4}{\text{day}}$$

**Step 6 (Bonus): Total Biogas Volume (assuming 65% CH₄)**

$$V_{\text{biogas}} = \frac{V_{\text{CH}_4}}{0.65} = \frac{437.5}{0.65} = 673.1 \;\tfrac{\text{m}^3\,\text{biogas}}{\text{day}}$$

### Final Tabulated Answer

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Digester Volume | $V$ | **833.3 m³** |
| HRT | $\text{HRT}$ | **8.33 days** |
| VS destroyed | $M_{VS,\text{dest}}$ | **1250 kg VS/day** |
| Methane produced | $V_{\text{CH}_4}$ | **437.5 m³/day** |
| Total biogas | $V_{\text{biogas}}$ | **673.1 m³/day** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Equivalent (Examiner's bonus mark):** Energy in 437.5 m³ CH₄/day ≈ 437.5 × 35.8 MJ/m³ = **15,663 MJ/day ≈ 4353 kWh/day** (since 1 m³ CH₄ ≈ 35.8 MJ ≈ 9.94 kWh).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Mesophilic vs. Thermophilic AD

| Parameter | Mesophilic AD | Thermophilic AD |
|---|---|---|
| Operating temperature | **30–40 °C** | **50–55 °C** |
| HRT (typical) | 15–30 days | 10–20 days |
| OLR tolerance | Lower (1–3 kg VS/m³·day) | Higher (2–5 kg VS/m³·day) |
| Pathogen kill | Partial | Excellent (> 99.9% coliforms) |
| Methanogen growth rate | Slower | ~2× faster |
| Stability to shock loads | More stable | More sensitive |
| Heating energy demand | Lower | Higher (15–25% more) |
| Net energy yield | Generally higher | Lower net (more heat loss) |
| KTU-recommended use | Indian municipal plants (most common) | Hospital / industrial biohazard waste |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Four-Stage Anaerobic Digestion Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["COMPLEX ORGANIC WASTE"]:::input --> B["STAGE 1 HYDROLYSIS"]
    B --> C["STAGE 2 ACIDOGENESIS"]
    C --> D["STAGE 3 ACETOGENESIS"]
    D --> E["STAGE 4 METHANOGENESIS"]
    E --> F["BIOGAS CH4 PLUS CO2"]:::output
    E --> G["STABILIZED DIGESTATE"]:::output

    B -.-> B1["Microbes: Clostridium Bacteroides"]
    C -.-> C1["Microbes: Acidogenic fermenters"]
    D -.-> D1["Microbes: Syntrophobacter Syntrophomonas"]
    E -.-> E1["Microbes: Methanosaeta Methanobacterium"]

    classDef input fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Parts of a Standard Mesophilic Anaerobic Digester

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["INLET FEED PIPE\nWITH GRINDER"]:::in --> P2["MIXING TANK\nOR PRE-DIGESTER"]
    P2 --> P3["MAIN DIGESTER TANK\nSEALED REACTOR"]:::core
    P3 --> P4["GAS COLLECTION DOME\nAND PRESSURE RELIEF"]
    P3 --> P5["GAS STORAGE HOLDER\nGASHOLDER"]:::gas
    P5 --> P6["GAS SCRUBBER\nH2S REMOVAL"]
    P6 --> P7["BOILER OR CHP UNIT\nFOR HEAT AND POWER"]
    P3 --> P8["HEAT EXCHANGER\nEXTERNAL SLUDGE HEATING"]
    P3 --> P9["MECHANICAL MIXER\nOR GAS RECIRCULATION"]
    P3 --> P10["EFFLUENT OVERFLOW\nWEIR AND SLUDGE DRAW-OFF"]
    P10 --> P11["DEWATERING UNIT\nCENTRIFUGE OR BELT PRESS"]
    P11 --> P12["LIQUID EFFLUENT\nTO TREATMENT"]:::out
    P11 --> P13["BIOSOLIDS CAKE\nLAND APPLICATION"]:::out

    classDef core fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef in fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef gas fill:#fde0e0,stroke:#8b0000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Functional Description of Each Digester Part

| # | Part Name | Function | Key Design Spec |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Feed Inlet + Grinder/Macerator** | Receives raw sludge, macerates large solids to ≤ 6 mm | Located below liquid level to prevent scum bridging |
| 2 | **Mixing Tank / Pre-digester (optional)** | Homogenization + pre-acidification, equalizes shock loads | 1–2 hours residence; reduces digester upset risk |
| 3 | **Main Digester Tank** | Sealed, gas-tight, oxygen-excluded reactor; houses 4 microbial stages | Cylindrical, **R:D = 1:1 to 2:1**; concrete or steel; insulated |
| 4 | **Heating System (External Heat Exchanger + Boiler)** | Maintains mesophilic (35 °C) or thermophilic (55 °C) regime | Heat loss = 5–10% of energy produced; spiral or tube-in-tube |
| 5 | **Mechanical / Gas Recirculation Mixer** | Prevents stratification, scum, grit, and dead zones | Gas mixing preferred (low maintenance); 30–60 min cycles |
| 6 | **Gas Collection Dome & Pressure Relief** | Collects biogas, prevents over-pressurization, prevents vacuum | Set at +5 to +10 inches water column; flame arrester mandatory |
| 7 | **Gas Holder / Storage** | Buffers fluctuating gas production; provides constant pressure to boiler/CHP | Double-membrane or floating-dome; sized for 4–8 hours storage |
| 8 | **H₂S Scrubber (Iron Sponge / Biological)** | Removes corrosive H₂S (typically 1000–3000 ppm from sewage gas) | Reduces to < 100 ppm; protects engines and pipes |
| 9 | **Combined Heat & Power (CHP) Unit** | Burns biogas → electricity + thermal energy for digester heating | Electrical efficiency 30–40%; thermal 50–55% |
| 10 | **Effluent Overflow & Sludge Draw-off** | Continuous removal of digested sludge at near-equal rate to feed | At center (deeper) and side (clearer supernatant) |
| 11 | **Dewatering Unit** | Reduces water content of digestate from 95–97% to 70–80% TS | Centrifuge or belt-filter press; produces biosolids cake |
| 12 | **Gas Flow Meter + Sampling Port** | Quantifies biogas yield and CH₄/CO₂ composition | Orifice / thermal mass flow meter; online CH₄ IR sensor |
| 13 | **Pressure / Temperature / pH Sensors** | Real-time monitoring of digester health | SCADA / DCS linked; alarms on O₂ ingress, pH drop |

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Operational Monitoring & Control Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["SENSOR LAYER\nTemperature pH Pressure Gas Flow"]:::sensor --> S2["SCADA / DCS\nCONTROL UNIT"]:::control
    S2 --> S3["ACTUATOR LAYER\nHeater Mixer Valve Pump"]:::actuator
    S3 --> S4["DIGESTER PROCESS\nSTAGES 1 TO 4"]:::core
    S4 --> S1
    S2 --> S5["ALARM AND\nDATA LOGGING"]:::output

    classDef sensor fill:#fff0d6,stroke:#a0522d,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#d6e0ff,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#e6d6ff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "draw a neat labeled diagram of an anaerobic digester," you MUST include: (1) **Inlet** with grinder, (2) **Heating coil / heat exchanger**, (3) **Mixer**, (4) **Gas collection dome**, (5) **Gas holder with scrubber**, (6) **Effluent outlet**, (7) **Temperature/pH sensors**. Marks are awarded for **labels**, not artistic skill. A clean Mermaid-style block diagram is acceptable in KTU answer sheets.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define anaerobic digestion. List the four stages of anaerobic digestion in sequence and state the energy yield of the process. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** is a controlled biochemical decomposition of organic matter by a consortium of anaerobes in the complete absence of molecular oxygen, producing **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** and a **stabilized nutrient-rich digestate**.

The four sequential stages are:

1. **Hydrolysis** — Biopolymers (proteins, lipids, carbohydrates) → monomers
2. **Acidogenesis (Fermentation)** — Monomers → Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, CO₂
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs & alcohols → Acetate, H₂, CO₂
4. **Methanogenesis** — Acetate & H₂/CO₂ → **CH₄ + CO₂ (biogas)**

**Energy yield:** Approximately **0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg VS destroyed at STP** (≈ 13.9 MJ/m³ of biogas), giving an overall plant energy self-sufficiency of 60–80% with CHP recovery.

---

### Question 2: Why are methanogens considered the most sensitive and critical microbial group in AD? Mention any two specific inhibitors. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Methanogens belong to the domain **Archaea**, are **obligate strict anaerobes**, have **slow growth rates (doubling time 2–25 days)**, and have **narrow tolerance windows** for pH, temperature, and toxicants. They govern the terminal and rate-controlling step of biogas generation, so any stress rapidly halts methane production and causes digester "souring" (propionate + VFA accumulation).

**Two specific inhibitors:**

1. **Free Ammonia (NH₃)** > **1700 mg/L** (converts to toxic NH₄⁺/NH₃ equilibrium).
2. **Hydrogen Sulphide (H₂S)** > **200 mg/L** (denatures iron-sulfur enzymes).
3. *(Other valid: heavy metals Cu, Zn, Ni, Cr; oxygen ingress; sudden temperature shock > 2 °C/day).*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`(a)` Explain in detail the four stages of anaerobic digestion. For each stage, state the substrate, microorganisms involved, and end products. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer Outline:**

| Stage | Substrate (In) | Microorganisms | End Products (Out) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Hydrolysis** | Proteins, Lipids, Polysaccharides, Nucleic acids | *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Streptococcus* (fermentative hydrolytic bacteria) | Amino acids, fatty acids, sugars, glycerol |
| **2. Acidogenesis (Fermentation)** | Monomers from Stage 1 | *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Desulfovibrio* (acidogenic fermenters) | VFAs (acetate, propionate, butyrate), H₂, CO₂, alcohols, lactate |
| **3. Acetogenesis** | VFAs and alcohols | *Syntrophobacter wolinii*, *Syntrophomonas wolfei* (Obligate Proton Reducers) | Acetate (CH₃COOH), H₂, CO₂ |
| **4. Methanogenesis** | Acetate, H₂/CO₂, formate | *Methanosaeta*, *Methanosarcina* (acetoclastic, 70%); *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus* (hydrogenotrophic, 30%) | **CH₄ + CO₂ (biogas)** |

*Add: Stoichiometric reactions, mention of syntrophy in Stage 3, pH control criticality.*

**`(b)` Describe the main functional parts of a standard mesophilic anaerobic digester with the help of a neat labeled diagram. Discuss the role of the gas collection and H₂S scrubbing system. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Main Parts of a Mesophilic Anaerobic Digester (List & 1-line function each — 4 marks):**

1. **Feed Inlet with Grinder/Macerator** — Reduces particle size; prevents clogging.
2. **Mixing Tank / Pre-digester** — Homogenization and shock-load buffering.
3. **Main Digester Tank** — Sealed, gas-tight, insulated concrete/steel reactor housing 4 microbial stages.
4. **Heating System (External Heat Exchanger + Boiler)** — Maintains 35 ± 2 °C mesophilic regime.
5. **Mechanical / Gas Recirculation Mixer** — Prevents scum, grit, stratification.
6. **Gas Collection Dome with Pressure Relief** — Captures biogas; sets +5 to +10 inches WC.
7. **Gas Holder** — Buffers production; supplies steady feed to CHP.
8. **H₂S Scrubber** — Removes corrosive H₂S; protects downstream equipment.
9. **Combined Heat & Power (CHP) Unit** — Burns biogas → electricity + heat.
10. **Effluent Overflow & Sludge Draw-off** — Continuous discharge; maintains steady volume.
11. **Dewatering Unit** — Reduces digestate moisture; produces biosolids cake.

**Role of Gas Collection and H₂S Scrubbing (3 marks):**

- **Gas Collection Dome:** Captures the biogas (55–70% CH₄, 30–45% CO₂, ~0.1–0.3% H₂S) at low positive pressure (5–10 inches water column). Prevents atmospheric release of potent greenhouse gas (CH₄ has 25× GWP of CO₂). Routes gas to holder → scrubber → CHP.
- **H₂S Scrubber:** Raw biogas from sewage sludge contains 1000–3000 ppm H₂S. Above 100 ppm, H₂S:
  - Corrodes engine parts, pipes, and heat exchangers (forms H₂SO₄ condensate).
  - Catalyzes SO₂ emissions on combustion (acid rain, public health hazard).
- **Scrubbing Methods:** Iron-sponge (Fe₂O₃ + H₂S → FeS + H₂O); biological thiobacillus biofilter; activated carbon; water scrubbing.
- **Output:** Biogas with H₂S < 50–100 ppm, ready for CHP or boiler.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`(a)` With the help of a mass balance, derive the relationship between digester volume, HRT, OLR, and feed concentration for a CSTR anaerobic digester. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer (Complete Derivation):**

**Given:** Volumetric feed $Q$ (m³/day), influent VS $S_0$ (kg VS/m³), digester volume $V$ (m³).

**Step 1 — Define HRT:**

$$\text{HRT} = \frac{V}{Q} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V = Q \cdot \text{HRT}$$

**Step 2 — Define OLR (Volumetric Organic Loading Rate):**

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{\text{Mass VS fed per day}}{\text{Digester Volume}} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V = Q \cdot \text{HRT}$:**

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{Q \cdot \text{HRT}} = \frac{S_0}{\text{HRT}}$$

**Step 4 — Mass-Balance on VS at Steady State:**

$$0 = Q \cdot S_0 - Q \cdot S - r_{\text{cons}} \cdot V$$

**Step 5 — Monod Kinetics for VS Consumption:**

$$r_{\text{cons}} = \frac{k_{\max} \cdot S \cdot X}{K_s + S}$$

**Step 6 — Combining, and assuming $S \ll K_s$ (substrate-limited, fibrous waste):**

$$Q (S_0 - S) \approx \frac{k_{\max} \cdot S \cdot X}{K_s} \cdot V$$

This yields the operating relationship used in KTU numericals.

**`Final Unified Equation (KTU Memory Hook):`**

$$\boxed{V = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{\text{OLR}} = Q \cdot \text{HRT}}$$

**`And the destruction efficiency:`**

$$\eta_{VS} = \frac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100 = 1 - \frac{S}{S_0}$$

---

**`(b)` A municipal treatment plant generates 80 m³/day of mixed primary sludge with VS = 30 kg/m³. The digester is operated at HRT = 20 days, and VS reduction efficiency is 50%. Calculate: (i) Digester volume, (ii) OLR, (iii) Daily VS destroyed, and (iv) Daily methane generated at STP (assume 0.35 m³ CH₄/kg VS destroyed). (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Given:** $Q = 80 \,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$, $S_0 = 30 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\text{HRT} = 20 \,\text{days}$, $\eta_{VS} = 0.50$, methane yield $Y = 0.35 \,\text{m}^3/\text{kg VS}$.

**(i) Digester Volume:**

$$V = Q \times \text{HRT} = 80 \times 20 = \mathbf{1600 \,\text{m}^3}$$

**(ii) OLR:**

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{S_0}{\text{HRT}} = \frac{30}{20} = \mathbf{1.5 \,\tfrac{\text{kg VS}}{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{day}}}$$

**(iii) Daily VS Destroyed:**

$$M_{VS,\text{in}} = Q \cdot S_0 = 80 \times 30 = 2400 \,\tfrac{\text{kg VS}}{\text{day}}$$

$$M_{VS,\text{dest}} = 0.50 \times 2400 = \mathbf{1200 \,\tfrac{\text{kg VS}}{\text{day}}}$$

**(iv) Daily Methane at STP:**

$$V_{\text{CH}_4} = Y \times M_{VS,\text{dest}} = 0.35 \times 1200 = \mathbf{420 \,\tfrac{\text{m}^3 \text{CH}_4}{\text{day}}}$$

**Bonus (Total Biogas at 65% CH₄):**

$$V_{\text{biogas}} = \frac{420}{0.65} = 646.2 \,\tfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Lose-Mark Zones):**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — KTU examiners **strictly deduct 0.5 marks** for missing m³, kg, or days.
> 2. **Mixing up HRT and SRT** — HRT is for liquids; SRT is for biomass retention. CSTR with no recycle has HRT = SRT.
> 3. **Not commenting on operational feasibility** — Always state whether HRT/OLR fall in the mesophilic safe range (HRT 15–40 d, OLR 1.6–4.8 kg VS/m³·d). Failure to do so costs **1 mark of "engineering judgment"**.
> 4. **Not converting to STP** for gas volumes — Examiners want volumes **at STP (0 °C, 1 atm)** unless otherwise stated.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** in digester parts — A **labeled block diagram is mandatory**; a written list alone is **incomplete**.
> 6. **Confusing "methanogens" with bacteria** — They belong to **Archaea**, a separate domain. Examiners explicitly test this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Anaerobic Digestion Rapid-Fire Revision Block**

- **Definition:** Anaerobic digestion = biological breakdown of organics **without O₂** → biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) + digestate.
- **Four Stages (in order):** **H**ydrolysis → **A**cidogenesis → **A**cetogenesis → **M**ethanogenesis (**HAAM**).
- **Rate-limiting step (high-cellulose waste):** **Hydrolysis**; (high-VFA waste): **Acetogenesis** (propionate).
- **Key Syntrophy:** Acetogens + hydrogenotrophic methanogens co-exist via **interspecies H₂ transfer** to keep $P_{\text{H}_2} < 10^{-4}$ atm.
- **Methanogens are Archaea**, not bacteria. ~70% CH₄ from acetate; ~30% from H₂/CO₂.
- **C/N ideal ratio:** **20:1 to 30:1** (by mass).
- **Mesophilic regime:** **30–40 °C** (commonly 35 °C); **Thermophilic:** **50–55 °C** (commonly 55 °C).
- **HRT (Mesophilic):** 15–40 days; **OLR:** 1.6–4.8 kg VS/m³·day.
- **VS reduction efficiency:** **40–60%** (mesophilic), up to 70% (thermophilic).
- **Biogas yield:** **0.3–0.5 m³/kg VS destroyed**; **~0.35 m³ CH₄/kg VS destroyed**.
- **Biogas composition:** **55–70% CH₄, 30–45% CO₂, trace H₂S**.
- **Sour digester diagnosis:** Falling pH (< 6.5), rising VFA (> 2000 mg/L as acetate), falling CH₄%, rising CO₂%.
- **Major Digester Parts (must memorize 8+):** Inlet grinder → pre-digester → main tank → heater → mixer → gas dome → gasholder → H₂S scrubber → CHP → effluent draw-off → dewatering.
- **Critical Inhibitors of Methanogens:** O₂, pH < 6.5, NH₃ > 1700 mg/L, H₂S > 200 mg/L, heavy metals (Cu, Zn, Ni, Cr), sudden temperature drop > 2 °C/day.
- **Co-digestion:** Blending N-rich and C-rich wastes to reach C/N ≈ 25:1; common KTU numerical topic.
- **Buswell's equation:** Stoichiometric formula for predicting CH₄ and CO₂ from empirical organic formula.
- **CHP (Combined Heat & Power):** Electrical efficiency 30–40%; thermal 50–55%; converts biogas to usable energy.
- **CO₂ is 25× weaker GHG than CH₄** — never vent raw biogas to atmosphere; always flare or recover.
- **Top KTU-memorized numerical:** $V = Q \times \text{HRT} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{\text{OLR}}$; $\eta_{VS} = 1 - S/S_0$; $V_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.35 \times M_{VS,\text{dest}}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
